coredata.dc:title: Luminescent properties of carbon dots originated from pine pollen for anti-counterfeiting application
coredata.dc:creator: Hong, Woo Tae
Yang, Hyun Kyoung
coredata.dc:description: A facile and green approach for the synthesis of luminescent carbon dots (CDs) was studied using pine pollen as raw material. The as-synthesized CDs exhibited hydrophilic properties and excitation-dependent emission owing to the presence of various ligands in the CDs. The luminescence mechanism of the CDs was studied via ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption and photoluminescence spectroscopy. Under 354 nm excitation, the CDs showed a maximum emission intensity at 438 nm, which makes it a suitable luminescent material for anti-counterfeiting applications. Based on these results, the development of the CDs through inkjet printing for anti-counterfeiting applications was studied. The printed patterns emitted blue light under UV excitation with a quick dry speed, high resolution, and excellent covertness under daylight. These new fluorescent CDs are promising candidates for luminescent materials used in anti-counterfeiting applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Pine pollen
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and structural properties of CDs
Absorption and luminescent properties of CDs

This work was also supported by BK21 FOUR (Fostering Outstanding Universities for Research) grant funded by the Korea government (Ministry of Education).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of preparation of CDs solution and anti-counterfeiting application of CDs using an inkjet printing.
(a) Low and high resolution (inset data) TEM images, (b) Gaussian fitting of size distribution (based on TEM image) and (c) XRD patterns of CDs.
XPS survey spectra in (a) Full range, (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) S2p, (e) O1s scan and (f) FT-IR spectra of CDs.
(a) UV–vis absorption, dominant PL and PLE spectra of CDs, (b) PL spectra of CDs for various excitation wavelength.
Comparison of objective pattern (letters, QR code, barcode) and the anti-counterfeiting pattern printed on non-fluorescent paper using the CDs ink under daylight and 365 nm LED lamp excitation.
(a) SEM and (b) high resolution SEM images of pristine paper. (c) SEM and (d) high resolution SEM images of printed paper.

head.ce:title: Luminescent properties of carbon dots originated from pine pollen for anti-counterfeiting application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Interdisciplinary Program of LED and Solid State Lighting Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan 48513, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Interdisciplinary Program of LED and Solid State Lighting Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Interdisciplinary Program of LED and Solid State Lighting Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan 48513, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical, Electronics and Software Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan 48547, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electrical, Electronics and Software Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan 48547, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile and green approach for the synthesis of luminescent carbon dots (CDs) was studied using pine pollen as raw material. The as-synthesized CDs exhibited hydrophilic properties and excitation-dependent emission owing to the presence of various ligands in the CDs. The luminescence mechanism of the CDs was studied via ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption and photoluminescence spectroscopy. Under 354 nm excitation, the CDs showed a maximum emission intensity at 438 nm, which makes it a suitable luminescent material for anti-counterfeiting applications. Based on these results, the development of the CDs through inkjet printing for anti-counterfeiting applications was studied. The printed patterns emitted blue light under UV excitation with a quick dry speed, high resolution, and excellent covertness under daylight. These new fluorescent CDs are promising candidates for luminescent materials used in anti-counterfeiting applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), defined as zero dimensional carbon nanomaterials with diameters below 10 nm, have been paid attention to many researcher as a new emerging fluorescent nanomaterials, due to their attractive advantages, such as high photo-stability, low toxicity, superior luminescence properties, high bio-compatibility, easy, and cheap preparation. Due to these advantages, application of CDs has been studied from LED, solar cell, bio-imaging, investigation of latent fingerprint, anti-counterfeiting, etc.

Among these applications, anti-counterfeiting application is ever-growing and important issue that affects customers, companies, and governments [1]. During past decades, magnetic response and plasmonic security labeling methods have been studied for anti-counterfeiting, and applied in banknotes, luxury items, tickets, public certificates, and documents [2-6]. Nevertheless, these methods have various disadvantages, such as high toxicity, complex utilization, and expensive cost. To solve these problems, the printing of luminescent materials has been suggested for anti-counterfeiting applications due to their diverse advantages, such as easy handling, high resolution, fast printing speed, diverse design, and versatile application to various objects [7,8]. In order to utilize printing of luminescent materials, choosing an optimum luminescent material, and printing method is necessary.

From the point of luminescent materials, utilizing conventional materials (chalcogenide quantum dots, and inorganic phosphors) have been critical problems, including poor dispersity in ink medium, expensive production cost, low photo-stability, high toxicity, and low covertness [9-11]. Owing to these problems, applying CDs is suggested as an alternative solution due to their upper-mentioned advantages. To enhance the anti-counterfeiting performance of CDs, the structural, morphological, and luminescent properties of CDs must be optimized. Also, large production yield and cheap production cost are necessary to increase applicability of CDs in the anti-counterfeiting field.

There are several synthetic carbon sources, and synthetic methods that have been utilized for CD precursors such as citric acid, folic acid, phenylenediamine, etc [12-14]. Although utilization of synthetic materials has high uniformity, reproducibility, and high quantum yield, the cost of synthetic resources is expensive, and their toxicity is harmful to the human body and environment. In opposition to synthetic materials, the use of recycled materials or natural organic sources have been studied as precursors for CDs, including sugar cane, durian, fruit juice, hair, honey, used toner powder, used frying oil, and coffee grounds, has been studied [9,15-21]. Compared with the synthetic materials, using a recycled or natural carbon source can reduce the production cost and environmental pollution. Among the natural carbon sources, we use the pine pollen as a precursor of CDs. Pine pollen causes allergic reactions, and reduces the transmittance of glass and solar cells; thus, its use, and recyclability is restricted [22]. To recycle the pine pollen, auto-fluorescent properties and surface modification of pine pollen was studied to apply in drug delivery and topical formulation of cancers, respectively [23,24]. However, these applications are not suitable for recycling on a large scale. Utilizing alpine pollen as a precursor of CDs is useful for homogeneous formation of CDs, due to their porous morphology. Moreover, this porous morphology assists in reducing the synthesis temperature and time when preparing CDs. Based on this, pine pollen can allow the affordable, and facile synthesis of CDs.

Printing method is also an important issue to decide anti-counterfeiting performance. In the printing method of luminescent materials, inkjet printing, spray coating, screen printing, and pen writing were studied [25-27]. Although spray coating, screen-printing, and pen writing exhibits easy handling, and potential in large scale, these methods behave low resolution, low covertness, and high difficulty to print complicated patterns, which reduces anti-counterfeiting performance [28]. In contrast with these methods, inkjet printing has various attractive benefits, such as high flexibility in the choice of structure design for printing complicated security patterns, easy handling, compatibility for large-area substrates, and high spatial resolution.

In this study, we report the solvothermal synthesis and anti-counterfeiting application of CDs originating from pine pollen. The blue-emitting CDs were synthesized by reacting pine pollen in an ethanol dispersion. To apply the CDs in anti-counterfeiting applications, CD ink was produced using an inkjet printer.

Pine pollen (Hunchun Xingli import and export trade co.ltd), sulfuric acid (Aldrich, 99.9%), ethanol (Daejung Chemical, 98%), and glycerol (Daejung Chemical, 99%) were purchased directly without any purification and cleaning process.

Pine pollen was used as the carbon precursor, while sulfuric acid was used as the oxidizing agent and catalyst for the solvothermal synthesis of the CDs. Pine pollen (1 g) was dispersed in 40 mL of ethanol with magnetic stirring. Sulfuric acid (1 mL) was added to the solution, the solution was transferred to a 60 mL Teflon vessel, and the vessel was sealed in a stainless-steel autoclave. The solvothermal synthesis was performed at 200 °C for 20 h. Subsequently, a transparent brown solution formed. The solution was purified by membrane filtration (regenerated cellulose ester filter, pore size: 0.22 μm), centrifugation (4000 rpm, 10 min), and sonication (10 min). Using a cellulose filter with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da, 40 mL of the solution was dialyzed to separate the CDs from the other organic components.

Liquid CD ink was prepared according to a previously reported method by mixing 10 mL of glycerol and 40 mL of CD solution under magnetic stirring. After preparation, the CD ink was injected into the ink cartridge (HP63) of an inkjet printer (HP Deskjet 2131). Anti-counterfeiting patterns were printed on non-fluorescent A4 paper. Photographs of the printed patterns were obtained using a digital camera (DSLR Canon EOS 100D) with a 5 mm lens (SIGMA MACRO, 50 mm, F2.8, EXDG) under daylight and 365 nm irradiation. The synthesis and application processes are summarized in Fig. 1 .

Morphological images of the CDs were obtained using a field-emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100F). The TEM specimens was prepared by depositing a drop of CDs solution onto a carbon-coated copper grid. Compositional and structural analyses of the CDs were conducted by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo VG Scientific, MultiLab 2000). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using an FTIR spectrometer (JASCO, FT/IR-6700). Raman spectra of the CDs were collected using a Raman spectrometer (JASCO, NRS-5100) with a 532 nm laser. The absorption properties of the CDs were measured using ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy (JASCO, V-670) and the photoluminescence spectra were obtained using a time-resolved spectrometer (PTI, PTI system).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structural properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 (a), (b), and (c) show the normal and high-resolution TEM images, size distribution, and XRD patterns of the CDs, respectively. A honeycomb lattice with a lattice distance of 0.22 nm was observed in the TEM images. This lattice structure is related to the characteristic (100) direction d-spacing of graphitic carbon. The average size of the CDs was calculated by fitting a single Gaussian function. The calculated average size was 3.77 nm, as shown in Fig. 2(b). The XRD patterns show two broad peaks at approximately 22.2° and 41°, which are related to the (002) and (100) directions of the graphite lattice, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2(c) [29].

The Raman spectra of the CDs are provided in Fig. 3 . The D and G bands are observed at 1345 and 1572 cm−1, respectively. The G band is related to the sp2 hybridized crystalline core, which originates from the graphitization of pine pollen. The D band originates from the defects and disordered carbon present in the CDs due to the surface ligand and oxidized surface of the CDs. Their intensity ratio (ID/IG) was 1.278. This value indicates that a two-dimensional crystal structure was formed with a highly carbonized composition.

Fig. 4 displays the (a) full ranged spectrum, high resolution (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) S2p, and (e) O1s XPS spectra, and the FTIR spectra of the CDs. The full range XPS spectrum shows the presence of C, N, S, and O atoms in the CDs. The C, N, S, and O atomic content is 42.24, 4.42, 4.16, and 49.18%, respectively. The Gaussian fitting of the C1s spectra is deconvoluted to three characteristic peaks at 284.98, 286.35, and 289.46 eV. These three peaks are related to C-C, C-N/C-S/C-O, and C = O bonding, respectively. The N-H (400.53 eV) and pyrrolic bonding (402.21 eV) are observed in Fig. 4(c). In Fig. 4(d), the S2p spectra are calculated as a sum of the two Gaussian peaks, which are related to C-SO4 (169.28 eV) and C-SO3 (170.57 eV) bonding. As a result of Gaussian deconvolution, the O1s spectra comprises two peaks, which are centered at 532.24 and 533.32 eV, as shown in Fig. 4(e). These peaks can be ascribed to C-OH/C-O-C and C = O bonding, respectively. The FTIR spectra show a broad absorption peak centered between 3230 and 3440 cm−1, which is due to the stretching vibrations of the –OH and –NH2 groups, respectively [30,31]. In addition, the existence of –OH and –NH groups causes the CDs to disperse in polar solvents, such as water, ethyl alcohol, etc. The four absorption peaks at 3068, 2975, 2920, and 1520 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of the C-H bonds in the aromatic rings [32]. The weak and broad absorption band centered at 2500 cm−1 is attributed to C-N and S-H bonds [33]. Absorption peaks were also detected at 1720 and 1625 cm−1, which correspond to the stretching vibrations of the C = O and C = C/C = N bonds in the aromatic ring, respectively. The existence of C-N and C-NH-C bonds is confirmed through the absorption peaks at 1426 and 1148 cm−1 [34]. The presence of a nitrogen dopant or amine ligand forms a surface state in the bandgap structure of the CDs and polarity, which results in high quantum yields and hydrophilic properties [35,36]. Finally, the absorption peak at 860 cm−1 originates from the out of plane bending of the C-H bonds in the aromatic ring [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption and luminescent properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 (a) and (b) contain the UV–vis absorption spectra, dominant PL and PLE spectra, and excitation-dependent PL spectra of the CDs. In Fig. 5(a), the absorption peak at 250 nm is related to a π– π * transition, which indicates that two-dimensional honeycomb lattice structure was formed in the CDs. The absorption peak at 290 nm is related to the absorption of graphene oxide owing to the strong oxidation of the CDs in ethanol [38]. The other absorption peak at 340 nm is related to a n– π * transition from C = O, C-S, and C-N bonding on the surface of the CDs. Fig. 5(a) shows that the CDs exhibit dominant emission at 438 nm under 354 nm excitation, which is suitable for blue luminescent anti-counterfeiting materials under UV excitation. As the electron-hole pairs are generated by excitation, the excitation from the ground state to the upper energy state is formed by a two-dimensional structure and oxidized graphene. Excited electrons are transferred to the lower energy state formed by C = O, C-S, and C-N bonding. The electron-hole pair recombines and emits blue light with an emission band centered at 438 nm. The emission spectra of the CDs show excitation-dependent emission in the 260 to 460 nm region, as shown in Fig. 5(b). The emission centers at each excitation wavelength are shifted to longer wavelengths owing to the existence of various ligands on the CD surface. According to previous reports, multi-chromophoric ligands can be controlled by the dipole moment of the solvent and the chemical composition of the precursor [39].

To apply the CD ink in anti-counterfeiting fields, high luminescent intensity and notable covertness of each substrate are required to disturb and distinguish counterfeiting. Selecting an anti-counterfeiting method and ink is also an important concern in the development of high-quality anti-counterfeiting techniques. Most valuable items that contain organic materials, such as fabric, banknotes, documents, and certificates, are adaptable to print anti-counterfeiting patterns. In this study, we printed an anti-counterfeiting pattern on non-fluorescent paper by adapting CDs to inkjet printing. Fig. 6 (a) and (b) show the printed letter pattern under daylight and 365 nm excitation, respectively, While Fig. 6(c) and (d) show the QR and barcode patterns under daylight and 365 nm light excitation. The printed patterns show high covertness in daylight, whereas clear blue emission appears under UV excitation, and high resolution, even when it is complex and delicate. The printing method has the advantage of flexibility in pattern design to produce complicated anti-counterfeiting patterns, where changes can easily be implemented using a software-based printer-control system. In addition, it is compatible with large areas and various substrates, such as paper, cotton polymers, etc. As ethanol is utilized as the solvent, the drying speed of the ink is faster than that of water-based ink; thus, the printed surface is less wrinkled and the CD ink less spread over the paper. This is advantageous for anti-counterfeiting applications.

To compare their thickness and surface morphology of pristine and printed paper, the SEM and high resolution SEM images of the pristine and printed paper were shown in Fig. 7 (a), (b), (c) and (d), respectively. The difference of thickness and surface morphology of paper was not observed. Since the CDs ink were permeated into the paper and chemical compound of paper, the anti-counterfeiting pattern can have excellent invisible properties.

CDs were synthesized from pine pollen through a solvothermal method. The facile, inexpensive, and sustainable synthesis was possible because of the abundance and low recycling ratio of pine pollen. The CDs exhibited an average size of 3.77 nm and a hexagonal lattice, which can be ascribed to the (100) direction of graphite. The Raman spectra indicated a two-dimensional honeycomb lattice and surface defects. The existence of various chemical bonds between the C, N, S, O, and H atoms was confirmed by XPS and FTIR analyses. The UV–vis spectra showed optical absorption peaks at 250, 297, and 342 nm, which originated from the sp2 carbon, graphene oxide, and surface defects, respectively. The CDs exhibited blue emission at 438 nm and excitation-dependent emission due to their two-dimensional structure and the presence of various surface ligands. The anti-counterfeiting capabilities of the CDs were investigated by using inkjet printing. The printed patterns exhibited structural design flexibility in the production of complex anti-counterfeiting patterns, where changes can be implemented using a software-based printer control system. In addition, this method can be used over large areas and on various substrate materials (such as paper, cotton, polyester, etc.). This method is a favorable candidate for application as a next-generation anti-counterfeiting technique.
Woo Tae Hong: Investigation, Writing–original draft, Visualization, Conceptualization. Hyun Kyoung Yang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT; Ministry of Science and ICT) (2020R1A2C400219311). This work was also supported by BK21 FOUR (Fostering Outstanding Universities for Research) grant funded by the Korea government (Ministry of Education).
